div.p: kαpaši kɔ̀la´ = violet. The root was used medicinally, but Sesostrie doesn't know how or what for.
ka´tan ta´sak = where is the dog?
kàta tàsak ʔu´ra` = where the dog lay down?
ka´ta ta´minuk ʔu´ra = where the cat lay down?
ka´ta to´nišik` = where is the man?
ka´tan to´niši´kun = where is the man?
kata to´niši´kun = where is the man?
ka´ta to´nihč ʔa´kin = where is the woman?
ka´ta pro-form to´ninu´hčihč n ʔa´kin v.chameleon
to´nihč ka´ta ʔa´kin` = where is the woman?
kàta to´nrɔwahkahč ʔàranˇ = where is the rice?
ka´ta ta´rihɔ̀hkawä´hanč ʔàraˇ = where is the cricket?
ka´ta pro-form ta´stihpùlak n ʔu´na´ v.chameleon
ta´stihpùlak ka´tʔuna = where is the star?
ta´hkamùčihč ka´tʔaran = where is the bread?
ta´tišumahč ka´tʔaranˇ = (also: ta´tišma) where is the meat?
kàta ta´rukɔ̀sak ʔùran = where is the hickory-stick?
ka´ta ta´rkuhč ʔa´ra` = where is the wood?
ka´ta ta´rkuk ʔu´ran = where is the wood? (same thing, only different word, says Sesostrie)
ta´rkuya´honik ka´ta ʔu´ran = where is the stick?
ka´ta ta´rkuk ʔu´ran = where is the log?
ka´ta ta´rkusin na´ʔara` = where are the logs? (le lɔg)
ta´rkuk ʔupòhtuwìhkin´ = you cutting the tree, a tree?
ka´ta ti´hkihč ʔa´ranˇ = where is the bayou? (understandable)
ka´ta ti´tihtʔäk ʔu´ranˇ = where is the river?
ka´ta ti´tihk ʔata´pärak ʔu´ra = where is the branch?
ka´ta ti´tihkòhok ʔu´ran = where is the little bayou?
ka´ta ti´tihkik ʔu´ran ti´tihkik ka´tʔuranˇ = where is the bayou? (better form)
ka´ta ta´yanärahč ʔa´ran = where is the ocean? (sounds better)
ka´ta ta´yanärah ʔu´ran = where is the ocean? (volunteered not so good)
ta´kafihč ka´tʔaran = where is the coffee? (also said: ta´kafi)
ka´ta ta´hkirihč ʔàran = where is the cornmeal?
ka´ta ta´hkatòmuhč ʔa´ran = where is the flour?
ka´tan ka´ta ta´yanišihč ʔa´kin = where is the cow?
ka´ta ta´yanišɔ̀wahč ʔa´kin = where is the cow?
ka´ta ta´stʔäk ʔu´hkin` = where is the horse?
ka´ta ta´stʔähč ʔa´kin` = where is the mare?
ka´ta ta´rihč ka´lʔäran´ = where is the house?
ka´ta ta´hahkarihč ka´lʔäran´ = where is the crib?
ka´ta ta´kwatok ʔu´na` = where is the bird?
ka´ta ta´rkuk ka´lʔuranˇ = where is the tree?
ka´ta te´kšak ka´lʔuranˇ = where is the pine?
kata ta´rku ʔɔšta ʔe´luk ka´lʔuranˇ = where is the trolly?
ti´hčiluk hàrahkʔuna´ = the mocking-bird is singing
ta´hkwatòhok hàrahkʔuna´ = the bird is singing
ka´ta ta´herihč ʔa´ranˇ = where is the boat?
ka´ta ta´wišihč ʔàranˇ = where is the water?
ka´ta ta´wišä̀nahč ʔa´ra n = where is the lake?
ka´ta ta´rku mi´li ɔ´štak ka´lʔuranˇ = where is the cedar?
ʔɔ̀rαšta ka´t ʔaran = where is the leaf?
tùhurahč ka´tʔaranˇ = where is the hill?
ka´ta tu´hurahč ʔa´ran` = where is the hill?
ka´ta ʔo´nerč ʔa´ranˇ = where are the horns?
ka´ta ta´ʔoner sa´hkun ʔu´ranˇ = where is the horn? (corrected to ʔo´ner)
ka´ta ta´ʔoner ʔi´li ʔu´ranaˇ = where are the two horns?
ʔe´yuh č ka´tʔaranˇ = where is the arm?
ʔi´hken č kàtʔaranˇ = where is the hand?
ʔi´hkenwùli ka´ta na´ʔaranˇ = where is the hand? (better)
ʔi´hkenwùlisìniman nàʔara = the fingers are lying (good: suggested by some)
ta´rk u ʔunimα ka´lʔurana = the trees (d.) are standing, lento with (u); allegro without
ta´šihkalʔùnima ʔùrana´ = the rocks (d.) are lying
ta´ritàmuhtu k ka´tʔuran = where is the broom?
tɔ´ndetišihč ka´tʔaranˇ = where is the milk?
to´niši ka´tʔuna' = where is the man?
to´nišik ka´tʔuhkinˇ = where is the man?
to´nišik ka´tʔuna = where is the man at?
to´nišik ka´tʔuran = where is the man lying down?
to´nihč ka´tʔaran = where is the woman?
to´nih ka´tʔaran = where is the woman?
ta´nisàrah ka´tʔačin = where is the girl?
ta´nisàra k ka´tʔuna' = where is the boy?
hä´ma rɔ̀hpan ʔùna´ = he's sitting close to you (f.)
ka´ta ta´kuhkuk = where is the turtle?
ka´ta ta´namihč na´ʔaraˇ = where are the lice?
ka´ta ta´kuhkuk ʔu´na = where is the turtle?
ka´ta ta´kuhkusìnima na´ʔaran = where are the turtles?
ka´ta taýimašuh ta´yitàmašu na´ʔaran = where are the matches: also [h]
ta´yiwoh(č) ka´tʔaran = where is the fire?
ta´škalàhpih ka´tʔaran = where are the shoes?
ʔe´yu ʔi´lin ka´t ʔurana' = where are my arms?
ʔe´yusınıman ka´ta na´ʔaran = where are several arms? (anybody's)
ʔe´yu ʔi´lin ka´ta na´ʔurana = where are my arms?
ʔä´ška ka´tʔaran = where are my feet?
ʔi´nih č ka´tʔaran = where are my teeth? [also: ʔi´ni]
ä´lαkaš ka´tʔaran = where is my hair?
ä´lαkàšihč ʔà˳ra´ = my hair is laying
lɔ̀tʔuhki = he's been running, il curü
lɔ̀taku´ = he's running; il a curü
lɔ̀tawi´ = he ran (il a curü u tʌ)
ti´hčin ʔači´ ʔa˳či´ tìhčʔači´ = she's there, she's sitting down
lɔ̀tʔači´ = she sat down and run
ta´yiwok = like you make a fire
the fire in fire-place could be called either
ta´payitak ta´rku the woods n ha´yihta
pa´tawihč ʔä´mʔaki´ wa´nʔaki ʔä´ma wa´nʔaki = when lightening strikes a tree, it burns
lɔ̀hkα´tàkuna´ lɔ̀hkʔɔsa´ = basket with a handle
ta´rku ta´rihku ʔi´li ka´lʔurana = the two trees are standing
ʔo´ni people ʔi´li cardnum ʔa´hakʔihč (apparently sic) ʔe´nihku lɔ´ta-nàʔara
nàmu sàčiku sàčihkʔùra´ = it's raining hard

ru´satʔä (mi´li´) = "red jack", has a red root, thick like potatoes, and when you boil it the water is red, red. Used as a medicine - for bowel diseases - good for purifying the blood, "thin it out" (says N.)
tàsɔ´mali k kàtʔuna´ = where is the flea?
tàsɔmalisìnima kàta nàʔaran = where are the fleas?
ta´kwatòhok ka´tʔuhkin ka´tʔuna´ = where is the bird?
both good ta´kwatosìnima (sic) kàta pro-form nàʔaran / ʔùkʔäran
ta´rihkuk ˇ ta´rkuk ˇ kàlʔura´ = there is a tree
ta´rikč ka´ta ka´lʔäranˇ = where is the house?
ka´ta pro-form ta´ri the house n ʔili cardnum kalʔurana / kàlisìrana (maybe sound a little better)
ʔu´ha´yišik' ʔu´ha´yik' ha´yišik' = "the one above", "the Lord", "the old man"
In old times the fire was a person; he could
turn any way he want, woman or man.
ta´hčʔi sun, the sun, was a girl
ta´hčʔa, the moon, was a " granny ".
The thunder was a person, naturally a
was the manager of the clouds and the
it gets to be the new year.
he must be crossing the world from pole to pole.
like a man, and makes a noise
he can hear them most any
time if he watches for him at
The eagle was a person - he was Tunica
They claim they (the 2 eagles) used to eat
one another, Sesostrie doesn't know whether it's
" put " them that way, maybe for
ta´šʔälamanˇ ʔuya´kata = they make him miserable, i.e., "punish him".
ta´lunti´tihkiš on Bayou des Glaise ʔu´ʔuna´ni he used to live.
ta´lʔukin kìču´ = in the town (dans village)
hana´hkuhč` hòt all ʔŭli´hčaniʔakä´ni` he got wet.
ta´lun ta´lunah ta´lunih ka´tʔaram = where is the field? "little town"
ka´ta ta´lʔukinˇ = where is the town?
ta´tama hɔ´hka n ka´t where pro-form (h conn) opi´ʔɔntǎč,
Volsin Chiki was not the real chief, but was appointed
chief till the boys grew up - then the oldest brother (Sesostrie's) did,
and that left Sesostrie to be chief.
Sesostrie got to be chief in 1911.
Alexander would have chief, instead of Volsin, but he was in Oklahoma.
Volsin čiki called Sesostrie - ʔèhtohku´ nephew
ʔètohku hč = (Sesostrie called Alexandre's daughter this, Sesostrie called Marceline's daughter this)
father's father's father mi´lakan Youchigant - Madane Wika alias Margerite Veure Melacon
father's father : James Youchigant - doesn't know
Sesostrie's father's sisters : Celéna, Marie Youchigant
ʔità̍tʔäku = my brother (man speaking, also woman)
tä̀raškɔ̀ta hč kata ka´lʔäran = where is the cabbage
She (ti´šlina) was glad he came - she
thought she was going to eat him that night.
da ʔarhilʔahtan da nikɔ´ni he said v.I.
ta´hčʔa rɔ̀wa hč = May ("must be"), better with suffix
kàyi hč = June ("must be"), better with suffix
mìli hč = July ("must be"), better without suffix
rùwina hč = August, better with suffix
Sesostrie thinks mi´li in July stands for " the hot "
Sesostrie thinks could not understand why they months are called thus.
ha´lʔupiš i na´mu´ = it's very muddy (= there's lots of mud)
sàči nàmu yàku = it rained "pretty much"
Alexandre Youchigant was raised at Evergreen
also possible : ta´nisara ʔe´nihku ran ʔuhki v.chameleon.....
ta´nisarasi´niman lɔ´tasiti´ = the girls are running
ta´nisarasä´man lɔ´takata´ = the boys are running
ta´nisarasi´nim a lɔ´tana´ʔara = the girls are running
te´šunʔàhkini = I'm eating breakfast (said also: te´šukani)
ya´kʔatan = come! (f.) - here, better than both the other forms, more polite
ya´kʔahčan = to a woman You must come.
ʔiya´ši wihki = you make me mad
ʔisä̀ri = cuss word (I hate you) (not said to a child) (use this word when you're mad)
hisa´pakiʔahča = I'm going to whip you (to female)
ʔisàri pànu´ man yàkapoʔin = I hate you, come here! (pa´na man is one phrase?)
sol, sol, sol, mi, fa, sol, [ more or less sung ], [ mi, do spoken ]
ʔu´w hin ya´wihč tiki´htʔähkin = He push me, so I pinched her. He make me that way, I pinched her.
ʔuta´pʔiman ʔuhi´šʔiki = catch him and push him.
(or) mi, fa, sol, mi fa, sol, mi, do.
lɔ´tʔahatan = don't run (to m. or f.)
lɔ´taya´kapo ʔin = come running! (to a child)
wä̀sapoʔin = (to a dog) try to jump
ma ʔišʔä´pʔaha´ lɔ´tʔiki = I don't like you, run!
ʔukä´hčawan ya´ʔahkin = I tried to bite him.
šu´rupowan ya´kan = I'm trying to smoke?
šu´rupo ʔi´wana šu´ru ʔi´wana = I wan tto try to smoke
ta´kuhpun ʔuku´hpunʔuhkä´ni = he put the bridle on (a horse)
ti´tihtʔä ʔu´ra v.chameleon ʔi´yanʔa where we go
ka´tan ta´yimuhkèrihč = where is the steamboat?
ta´minuk mèli'´ = the cat is black
ta´minuk kɔ̀ta' = the cat is gray
ta´minuk ri´sakɔ̀ta' = the cat is gray-striped
ka´ta ta´minu mèlikᵘ = where is the black cat?
ʔɔ´kaši ʔo´sik ru´šta rɔ̀wa ʔura´pkʔuhki = the boy's father killed a white rabbit
hi´nahkᵘ = is it that way? (short way)
hi´lʔatan´ = wait! (to either m. or w.) (?) (don't move!)
wi´niman winšʔä̀pa´ = you are happy m. d. (can't pronounce it)
hi´niman hinšʔä̀pa´ = you are happy f. d. (can't pronounce it)
si´niman sinšʔä̀pa´ = they are happy (f. d.)
ʔaha´ làputan´ làputαn´ = it might be good
ʔi´man ʔipi´tʔɔki tarkukičun` = I'm lost in the woods
sa´kʔunihkʔùnana = They done eating, i.e. they have eaten
ta´ri hàyiši´ = the top of the house
ka´ta ta´hkɔrahč = where is the table?
ka´ta ta´rihkɔ̀ra ka´lʔäran = where is the wagon standing?
ka´ta ta´hkɔra ka´lʔäran = where is the table?
ta´hkɔra ka´ta ka´lʔura = where is the table?
also : ta´ri the house n kà · i ha´yiht
hiatus in kà · i, in place of h
also : ʔo´rusʔa · ale´yuta´ni (with hiatus for h.)
also : ka´šile · e ʔùwana´ (with hiatus for h.)
ka´tan ta´rupohčˇ = where is the dream?
[ ʔa´rupok; can't say that, that's too short.]
yu´ka ti´ponikʔàhčaˇ = I'm going to see her
ti´poʔànikʔàhčaˇ = I'm going to see her
yu´ka tìpo´ʔìwana´ = I want to go see her
ka´ta ta´škalahpi tä´lahču yu´ruhč = where is the book? (??) boots?
kato´tu nˇ tèmilä´ni = it's red everywhere
kato´tu nˇ tèmili´ nùhči = it's red everywhere
kato´tu tèʔɔšta´ = it's blue all about
kato´tu tèrɔwa´ = it's white all about
kato´tu mèli = it's black all about
kato´tu tèmeli´ = it's black all about
[ -lɔ is stressed pretty heavily but is low.]
hi´šta · àhki´ with hiatus instead of h
ka´ta tɔ̀hkatʔäku = where is the mallard?
ka´ta ta´ tɔ̀hkahč = where is the she-mallard?
ka´ta ta´kayinaku = where is the goose?
ka´ta ta´kayinahč = where is the she-goose?
ka´ta tìtihtʔäku ta´- = where is the river?
ka´ta tìtihkiku = where is the bayou?
ka´ta ʔo´tisäma = where are his pants?
ka´ta ta´yanišɔ̀wahč = where is the cow?
ti´hčinˇ lɔ´tatìwanaˇ = she wants to run
lɔ̀tʔuwànʔaha^ = he doesn't want to run
There was a place where they ised to
Those Choctaw that used to live here
in Louisiana went to ska´li tu´klu, Oklahoma.
His (Sesostrie's) cousin live near Coalgate, Oklahoma.
There used to be some Choctaw living
at Oberlin, Louisiana, north of Kinder --- one lives now at Oakdale, one lives at Glenncore
They've moved and gone to Oklahoma, live in Choctaw Nation, there were 3 or 4 tribes here.
There used to be some Indians living
on an island, in Natchitoches Parish
to the Red River, he thinks.
between Red River and Old River, they call it.
Some Indians used to live near
Caddo Lake, but it must be they went to
They found oil there, and the
found some law, and they claim that
living back of Natchitoches - not so
Sesostrie has heard of some Indians living
below New Orleans, near the point, but he doesn't know just where
Some Choctaw living at Trout, Louisiana,
84, some of these used to live between Lecompte and Woodworth.
Sesostrie doesn't know where they lived.
Sesostrie has heard of the Pascagoula
Sesostrie has heard of the Apalachee
Sesostrie has heard of the Okelousa
Sesostrie heard that some tribe was weak,
weak, but they disappeared and nobody
eat " grub ", but they cooked meat and drank
Sesostrie has heard of the ta´nšipàho´
of lake Ponchartrain, close to the
Some Indians are living near Saint Gabriel,
below Baton Rouge - doesn't know what kind.
Sesostrie has heard of the Mugalasha.
Sesostrie has heard of the Bayougoula.
se ruž (unvoiced off glide) = mìli adj pàna´
ą̈ta´ de´ze´glìz = a whole lot of churches
(ž seems to be in the Tnuica position of š.)
The place where the Avoyelles came out
was near Bayou Cypre, Cypress Bayou.
The owl he told : you'll be there
to let people know when somebody
faces, they made about three (2 or 3, says Sesostrie,
or " about 3 ") (now says 3) stripes on each side
They didn't use black, none at all.
Sesostrie never saw any white, either.
They used just yellow and red,
they use yellow together with the
red, but they didn't use yellow alone.
When they danced the Round Dance
(War Dance, it's about the same), that was
a signal to the other tribes to prepare for
Tunica men used feathers, but not the
Some used eagle feathers, some used
Sometimes just four or five on the band.
the Tunica wore just one feather in
the back, which stood up straight due
to the way it was fastened on the band.
The band was red; used red flannel
The Choctaw didn't paint when they played
ball, only when they dressed to dance.
horizontally on n the face, sometimes low
a winch to the side and under the eyes.
[ horizontal line ] was black, [ three vertical lines ] sometimes the middle was yellow, both sides was red
Likewise the Tunica did not paint
he wore a band with feathers in it and a
front of the head and in the back
Sesostrie thinks the Choctaw manner of painting
was a sign for something, but he doesn't
he doesn't know whether the Ofo
Sesostrie doesn't know when the Biloxi or the
here after the Tunica came here.
among the Tunica the father and mother
you-all do, the father helped raise the
if the children did anything wrong
they, father or mother, would take a whip
whip them the first time, but told
listened and did what they were told.
leg to get rid of bad blood.
this every time they wanted to play
ball - just the men were so treated.
The instrument used in doing this
They claim this would keep you from taking the
was the little leg-bone from the back leg
legs, starting at the side of the back of the leg and
scratch three times, the last scratch being
on the other side of the back of the leg.
The scratches were about six inches
long - the length from my thumb to
the end of my index finger stretched
they take a piece of peeled cane about
as wide as your finger, and
scrape down over the area once,
twice if you're bleeding pretty much.
is held with the two hands of the doctor one
on either side of the leg.
When you had a paint in your side,
or your head, arm, leg, back, the
doctor would take a little horn, and,
placing it over the affected area, would
The little horn was about 3cm, or a little
large whend was put over the afflicted area,
and the doctor sucked through the
sharp end, where a little hole was cut.
The doctor could be a man or a
They " got to learn how to do "
a year or two to learn how to be a doctor.
but Sesostrie doesn't know what affliction
make different kinds of linement, and the
doctor would rub you with that.
A strong runner of the Choctaw
wore a deer-tail fastened at the rear
ta´lɔhkahč the bottom of the basket
ha´luhta post wi´lanč weaving ho´tuhtan when I finish
ka´ta ta´lɔhkahč = where is the basket
ka´ta ta´lɔhpašihč = where is the basket
ka´ta ta´lɔhkah ka´lʔäran = where is the basket standing
they scratched the leg of the " strong runners "
" They tell me that when they (the
old Indians) would come to a river and
didn't have no boat, they would a log
into the river, and the log would
lay there on the other side.
tɔ̀hkatoh = cercelle (sic) something like a mallard, but smaller. In all probability, the green-winged teal.
kàyina = white-fronted goose, or gray goose
yàkʔɔšta = a blue heron agret blü (bleu)
Indians don't kill the woodcock they say
it's a sin - but Sesostrie doesn't know if anything
that would happen to you if you did.
If you kill the kingfisher, there's going to
nìma = great horned owl, horned owl
Hairy woodpecker - when it's cold, cold
he holler - also when company is going to come.
In spring he holler " wilu wilu "/ do do / ti do
The humming-bird turns worm in the fall -
he stays there in the ground all winter, and in
the spring he turns into a little bird again.
On seeing the nest he says : " I didn't know
One time, Sesostrie caught one of these
worms, hoping to see it turn into
a bird, but the day when he thought
it was going to turn, it disappeared.
na´hkatatapi ku´watoh = fly-catcher, literally, and possibly to be questioned ą̈ perar
ku´watohk ʔu´hniyupʔahatòhok kùwatohk ʔuhni´yupʔahatoh or ʔu´hniyupʔàhatòhok = ruby-crowned kinglet, probably
rùwišìyu´ = possibly orchard oriole, see page 147
(Sesostrie says the (Biloxi) women spoke differently from the
men - the had some different words)
wìnanikʔàhča = I'm going to hear it
wìhtanikʔàhča = I'm going to hear it
wi´witakʔàhča = you (m.) are going to hear it
wi´hätakʔàhčan = you (f.) are going to hear it
wi´ʔutakʔàhčan = he is going to hear it
wi´ʔatakʔàhča = she is going to hear it
ʔàyitàhulu = wood-thrush (thrushes, hermit, wood, and gray-checked, mostly like a wood-thrush)
do, do, re, do fa, do do, re do mi
ka ka gi a ri, ka ka gi a ri

the spring is open, there won't be no more cold "

to cross the Mississippi to kill them.

would catch deer in that way.
ʔàlatàwuči = cane whistle used as decoy

When you see a bear, you mustn't
look at him - " I guess that's insult to him,
ʔàhpasalàmìhta = a kind of animal that stays in the water
ka´ta taʔučähkahč = where is the frog
The root of the blackhair (related to withe-rod)
They make a tea out of the
They pull it up, wash it good, and
They used the bark of the
For the bow, they used hickory
kòhkučä̀ma´ = lives in the water, a small turtle
The old Indians used to say that
if you eat frog, the section will
get dry (and it look night, like
(big Mississippi very big yellow cat or yellow mud-cat, small)
fruit raw, when they're ripe - " that's good ".
The root is used as a medicine, but
čìpʔelu čìpu ʔèlu = fruit of the maypop
women made a tea for monthly pain.
ʔàyuhki = garfish (about big as a man's leg)
wàsa´ = another kind of garfish (4-5 feet long)
There's a kind of root, you can chop it
up and throw it in the water and
that poisons the fish, and the
fish will come to the top and you
can shoot them with a bow-and-arrow.
The French name of the root - de
in a boll (pod), and if you put some
seeds in your pocket, that's good
sä̀lahki rìhku sä̀lahki = thorn-tree (probabkly Ligladitsie Aquodatica)
Took coon guts and cleaned them
and dried them and they used
If they wanted to poison arrows
they stuck a bunch of them (lots
of them) into the flesh of a dead
take the arrows out, and if
It'll kill you - you can't cure
If an animal's blood (killed with a poisoned arrow)
touches your blood in an open wound,
rìhkusàyiča´ = dèʒagàsya´ (French), used to make bows with this, possibly bowdark
To make black dye, they mixed
with the nuts of the rùmeli.
kòyaša´ = tree that has little red seed

the bark of the blackjack oak, and the
(The blackjack doesn't grow here - it grows in pine woods)

are just like a gum tree ].
(Used to dye cane just black, red,

ka´ta ti´šuhkihč = where is the door?
hi´štahahk màhčikani´ = I'm still picking cotton
hìsawa´ = root used for dying cane red
They say that wren will count out your age
with appendage under chin, when he lights on your
Used to take the root of the " red Jack,"
and beat it up (rùsatʔä ʔēlu) → better
[ Ochre is hibiscus -- close to cotton.
Made a medicine from the root.
and then make a plaster to put
shoot a spirit -- used the stalk.
used the leaves to make a
Maybe they used to make a trap to catch them in, a
You can take the fluff of the thistle
To make a purge for a child, take
and put it warm milk and boil it --
when it's cold, you give it the child when
you peel the bark, it exudes a milk-
fresh lard, make a salve with
They boil the root for " bad disease."
(slippery elm) They boil the bark first goo, remove the bark and then they
mix it (the water) with fresh lard, and then
they boil it again with the lard
This makes a sort of salve.
once found a flat stone vessel,
four fingers think -- maybe a little thicker.
Hollowed out like this on both sides.
They used the hisawa to make red
the seeds and planted the seeds in the spring
grows just like a radish -- kinda yellow in color)
and the cane together all day
long every day for a week or two weeks --
until the cane was red, red, just like
at night the cane and roots were
left standing in the water ready to
be boiled again the next day.

The blackjack oak bark (bark not needed) and
were boiled together with the cane
for about 2 or 3 weeks or longer.
if it hasn't boiled good (if the
color hasn't taken properly) they boil

together with the cane for abuot 3 wks.

bark of the walnut are boiled together
with the cane for about 3 wks.

used to make them baskets and

says the old Indians claimed that
the Houma were good people -- S.
he thinks the Tunica used to live with
The Koasati use the leaeves of the
goldenrod (which doesn't grow here) to make
They use the stalk and leaves
(picked when it's green, before it dries up)
to make a tea -- drink it hot, stir
in sugar -- cold for a fresh cold.
has a " secret " cure for snake bite
The old Indians used to pray.
has heard them pray, but he was too small
to learn that in his language.
used to know God and the devil, just
Indians never used any kind of " sacrifice,"
has read about it, how they used to do so
The Tunica used to bury people " just
night with the body, and the
" guinea wasp " side -- they're mean, mean.
get you, even if you jump in the water
sàtʔäya´ = "little short," but the same thing
The guinea wasp makes a nest of paper in
They used to make a fire (to drive the spirits of the dead person away) for four
nights, and the morning after, early
plunge in the water four times
The whole tribe did this when somebody died.
for cousin for three or 4 months --
used to see it, they were
black -- what they did before that,
and washed their hair most every day.
one whose root reddens the water
when boiled -- the other's root whitens -- box elder
the water when boiled -- The red is
purifies the blood -- or for inflamed kidneys
A woman stayed a widow for a year
they didn't marry brother-in-law, sister-in-law -- that's
too close kin -- they had enough at
that time to choose who they wanted.
cousin -- " too close in the family."
you could marry your brother-in-law's or
introduced to this country) or Jimson Weed -- the leaves
are mashed up and fry in fresh lard --
then the drip it (drain the lard from the leaves)
and use the lard drippings when cool as
a salve to rub on the sore back of a horse.
The flowers of this plant are sweet in
the fall, and if a dog eat them

other Indians (take 'em in war) and make
ka´ta ta´tu´hurahč = where is the hill
ka´ta tu´hurak = where is the hill (about the same thing)
The old Indians say there was no Red
River -- it usd to be Bayou Boeuf
The Red River used not to be here.
swamp where the Red River is now.
say it used to be ocean -- but the
ocean dried up leaving a swamp there.
They (old Indians) cut a ditch there
the hill where the Red Riveer is now, and
every time it rained it washed
away until the Red River formed.
pi´rʔuhkä´ni ti´tihk a bayou n pi´rʔuhkä´ni` keep a-forming.
one of the arrowheads is shaped
The broken piece of a flint knife is shaped
They used to make beads from quartz -- S.
doesn't know what they cut it with.
doesn't know where they got the quartz.
it might they went to Arkansas for it --
says " they tell me v.I there's some there " --
going by boat up the Washita River.
has found big and small pieces
pieces as big as a large muskmelon --
these have all been " picked " now.
time it rains something washes out.
now only small broken pieces are to be
knows no word in Tunica for quarts.
has brought here two flint arrowheads
a piece of flint knife, and a piece of
these were found in the second
mound from his place (" kinda east "),
or a little more from his place --
people have picked up all the arrowheads
you can find pieces of pottery there.
They make some kind of tea for something
remembers bearing, when he was a
small boy, that the Tunica fought
with some tribe on n black River --
This weed grows to about 4 ft.
There is a certain weed (poss Jerusalem oak,
110 of flower-book -- or something similar), the
leaves of which one boils in about 1½ gals.
boiling it down to make about a gallon -- then about it
is boiled " good ", one puts it in a
on it (the hot water) -- it steams and
makes you sweat -- will cure you of piles
in a little or no time.
There is a vine having red flowers, round -- when you touch it the leaves and flowers curl up.
The root of this is boiled to make a tea for
You boil a good portion the root about a half hr.,
in about a gallon of water -- boil down to one quart.
You pour off the liquid and drink about
2½ or 3 fingers of this three times a day before
The liquid is red and doesn't have
where there is a flat place
If you're pretty bad sick with piles
you can used both of these treatments at
once, and " I'll guarantee " you'll soon
get over the piles -- " if you ain't born
The old midwife alwasy had a kind of
tea in the garden and would give the patient
Made a tea from a little bit of leaves and stalks
Peppermint tea is good for to drink at night when it's cold --
it's good for a cold -- if you'd drink that all the time you
The edge of the fanner Fr.
is made in the Choctaw fashion.
mother used three strands of cane, instead
The type of weaving found on n the
inside bottom of my double-weave Chit.
says his (mother's mother) grandmother said she'd
rather see a white (American or French) war than an
The Spanish were just as cruel as the Indians.
take the babies by the leg & knock
she see the star falling on the the ground.
You had to make a pillow of your
bread -- you never knew when you'd
" each nation had an animal name.
he knows are the Tunica's & the Biloxi's.
A rattlesnake isn't going to bite
you until he has notified you -- if you
get too close to him he makes a
rattling sound, then he bites you.
Biloxi met together, they were friends,
because the Tiger and the Rattlesnake
He thinks the Choctaw " goes by "
the Horned Owl, " if I ain't mistaken."
Why the Tunica and the Biloxi Became Friends
with the Spanish & with the french.
when the french had a war with
the Natchez, the Tunica helped the
helped the Spanish in a war with
it must be the Tunica came
here, and then the Biloxi came
she saw when the stars fell.
After that she saw the Jackson war.
The stars (comets) that fell were good-
sized, but if you'd go there and

Jim Thompson -- an old Indian living
Alec Bloss -- lives with Barefoot Wilson

Pine Prairie, south of Alexandria -- Indians living there

Words of song sung while playing the game.
i o a i a i e
pass the ball -- pass the shot
Chatot -- probably the remnant now at Kinder.
The old people used to sit around in
a circle and play this game -- they
passed the buckshot under the left
your knee with both hands and
hand under your knee while you
" It's not good you learn too much
pass or pretend to pass the shot
to the person next to you.
the shot or pretend to under your
right leg, and pass it on or pretend
They played this for fun, and
never saw them bet on it, but
he says they could have if they wanted
talatosuk I'm hiding ʔupi´húhkʔàtani´ the shot
ʔàha´ mištik ʔàni´ = I'm going a pine
ka´ʔaš yàkʔakʔahčą = what day you going to come back?
ʔàha´ sèhiti mìhta yàkanikʔahča = I'm coming back day after tomorrow
láwuš e´škų ràhyɔʔuhki = it thundered yesterday
hä´ɔ̨äšį ràhinʔuhki = it's been thundering today
la´wu´mi´hta ràhinʔuhki = Day before yesterday it thundered
se´hiti ràhʔutαkʔahčα = tomorrow it's going to thunder
häʔäš sàčʔuhki = it's been raining today
se´hiti sàčuwikʔahča = it's going to rain tomorrow
Heard they were almost the same tribe, when he was small.
says that the Avoyelles & the Natchez
could understand one another -- almost the
never heard this, just thought it by
himself -- didin't hear it from Sivt.
Biloxi chief -- bɔ srɔ̨, big name
Ofo chief -- old man su · r -- Pauline's mother's father
back of Lecompte, on n a hill, in
one of two Choctaw living at Oakdale, a
Josephine Paul live at Wiergate, Texas.
Henry Jackson's mother was full-blood Biloxi --
his father was half Choctaw - half Biloxi.
P's daddy was Choctaw, mother white.
Possibly used as a tea for women (ask Pauline)
Made a tea to drink when struck by lightning.
Also to bathe the eyes with.
old name for God; hutnehin, name for white man's God.
gito´ (gi´to) (named from a man whose name was this)
aBaskų´ a`Du´dinì = the bread is cooked
Gopher (large land tortoise -- good food -- 18 in.
makes holes in the sand banks, also makes
mounds of sand, where it scratches out the hole)
Les Nadchez on n les Cheloels.

" The old people were more plain " p. 82
la´puta´n it might be good p. 82
Put about 1 / 3 of a teasppon dry in
your mouth and drink some water.
If you have pains under it, take a few drops of purgoric.
